impacts.legalQuestion: Whether a patent holder's conditioning of patent licenses on customers' exclusive purchase of unpatented products constitutes unlawful tying under the Sherman Act, and whether the defense that the tied products are 'nonstaple goods' lacking substantial non-infringing uses shields the patent holder from antitrust liability.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed a jury verdict finding Ingevity liable for unlawful tying under federal antitrust law by conditioning licenses to its '844 patent on customers' exclusive purchase of Ingevity's unpatented carbon honeycomb products. The court upheld that the question of whether honeycomb products had substantial non-infringing uses was properly submitted to the jury, which found against Ingevity. The patent at issue had previously been held invalid.
impacts.positiveImplications: Clarifies that accused tying parties can raise the nonstaple goods defense, but the factual determination of whether products have substantial non-infringing uses is a jury question, providing defendants an opportunity to present evidence of limited uses.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders who condition licensing on exclusive purchase of unpatented products face significant antitrust exposure. Even after a patent is invalidated, prior licensing conduct can support tying claims with substantial damages.

impacts.caseName: Trustees of Columbia University v. Gen Digital Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patent claims directed to detecting anomalous program executions by comparing function calls to a model are abstract under step one of the Alice framework, and whether damages for patent infringement may be based on the patent holder's foreign sales.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit vacated a $185 million jury verdict for Columbia, holding that the asserted claims of the '322 and '115 patents are abstract at Alice step one and remanding for step two analysis. The court also held that the district court erred in allowing damages based on Norton's foreign sales and vacated enhanced damages and attorneys' fees tied to an improper contempt finding against Norton's counsel.
impacts.positiveImplications: Strengthens patent eligibility defenses for companies accused of infringing software patents directed to anomaly detection, and limits patentees' ability to recover damages based on foreign sales of accused products.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders asserting cybersecurity and software-related patents face heightened risk of invalidation under Alice § 101, and may not recover damages attributable to foreign sales.

impacts.caseName: GoTV Streaming, LLC v. Netflix, Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patent claims directed to server-side tailoring of content presentation specifications to a wireless device's screen size and capabilities are patent-eligible under 35 U.S.C. § 101.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit held that GoTV's patents claiming methods of tailoring content delivery to wireless device capabilities are ineligible under § 101, reversing the district court's judgment of infringement and $2.5 million damages award in favor of GoTV. The court concluded the claims are directed to an abstract idea, requiring entry of judgment for Netflix.
impacts.positiveImplications: Technology companies accused of infringing content-delivery and adaptive streaming patents gain a stronger basis to challenge such patents as abstract under § 101.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders in the content delivery and streaming technology space face increased vulnerability to § 101 challenges, potentially undermining portfolio value.

impacts.caseName: Global Tubing LLC v. Tenaris Coiled Tubes LLC
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether summary judgment was proper on claims of inequitable conduct in patent prosecution and Walker Process fraud for attempted monopolization when genuine disputes of material fact existed regarding the applicant's knowledge of and duty to disclose prior art.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit vacated summary judgment on both Tenaris's inequitable conduct in obtaining patents and Global Tubing's Walker Process antitrust fraud claim, finding genuine disputes of material fact on both sides. The case involves coiled tubing patents where the applicant allegedly failed to disclose prior CYMAX manufacturing documents acquired from a predecessor.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent challengers benefit from the court's willingness to send inequitable conduct and Walker Process fraud claims to trial where there are factual disputes about an applicant's knowledge of undisclosed prior art.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders face increased litigation risk where they obtain patents covering technology similar to prior art acquired through corporate acquisitions, as failure to disclose such art may support both unenforceability and antitrust claims.

impacts.caseName: REGENXBIO Inc. v. Sarepta Therapeutics, Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether claims directed to cultured host cells containing a recombinant nucleic acid molecule encoding an AAV capsid protein with a heterologous non-AAV sequence are patent-eligible under 35 U.S.C. § 101 or directed to a natural phenomenon.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed summary judgment of patent ineligibility, holding that claims to engineered host cells containing recombinant nucleic acid molecules encoding AAV capsid proteins are not directed to a natural phenomenon under § 101. The court found the claimed cultured host cells are human-made compositions markedly different from anything occurring in nature.
impacts.positiveImplications: Significant win for biotech and gene therapy companies, confirming that claims to engineered host cells with recombinant nucleic acid constructs are patent-eligible, protecting investments in AAV-based gene therapy platforms.
impacts.negativeImplications: Competitors in the gene therapy space face continued patent barriers, as engineered biological constructs receive strong patent eligibility protection from the Federal Circuit.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the PTO Director's instructions to the Board governing discretionary denial of inter partes review petitions constitute substantive rules requiring notice-and-comment rulemaking under 5 U.S.C. § 553, or are exempt general statements of policy.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed that the PTO Director's instructions for exercising discretion to decline IPR institution are general statements of policy exempt from notice-and-comment rulemaking under § 553(b). The court emphasized that Congress provided no legal right to IPR institution and that the Director retains ultimate authority over institution decisions.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders benefit from the PTO's flexibility to manage IPR institution through internal policy guidance without the constraints of formal rulemaking, reducing the risk that discretionary denials will be challenged procedurally.
impacts.negativeImplications: Companies relying on IPR to challenge patents face continued uncertainty as the PTO can adjust institution standards through informal guidance without public notice-and-comment, potentially limiting access to IPR proceedings.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the ITC correctly found that Apple violated Section 337 of the Tariff Act by importing Apple Watch devices incorporating blood oxygen measurement technology covered by Masimo's patents.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the ITC's finding that Apple violated Section 337 by importing Apple Watches with blood oxygen sensing features that infringed Masimo's patents, upholding the limited exclusion order barring importation of infringing models. The court found no error in the Commission's domestic industry determination, validity rulings, or infringement findings.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders with strong domestic industry investments gain confidence that the ITC remains a viable forum for enforcing patents against large-scale importers, with exclusion orders providing powerful remedies.
impacts.negativeImplications: Major technology companies integrating third-party patented technology into consumer products face significant risk of import bans through ITC enforcement, potentially disrupting product availability and supply chains.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court properly approved a $125 million class action settlement resolving claims that the federal judiciary overcharged PACER users by using fees to fund expenses unrelated to PACER's operation.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed approval of a $125 million nationwide class action settlement in the PACER fee overcharging case, rejecting an objector's challenges to subject matter jurisdiction, settlement fairness, attorneys' fees, and incentive awards. The court found that hundreds of thousands of PACER users will be fully or substantially reimbursed for fees paid during the eight-year class period.
impacts.positiveImplications: The settlement provides direct financial recovery to businesses and law firms that are heavy PACER users, and establishes that excessive government fees can be challenged through class actions under the Little Tucker Act.

impacts.caseName: 112 Genesee Street, LLC v. United States
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether restaurant owners who submitted timely applications to the Restaurant Revitalization Fund but did not receive grants due to the SBA's processing order have Tucker Act jurisdiction to sue for damages based on the mandatory statutory language directing grants 'in the order in which applications are received.'
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed that over 300 restaurants have Tucker Act jurisdiction to pursue claims for unpaid RRF grants, holding that the statute's use of 'shall award grants' and 'in the order in which applications are received' created a mandatory, money-mandating obligation. The court rejected the Government's motion to dismiss for lack of jurisdiction.
impacts.positiveImplications: Small businesses denied government grant funds due to agency processing errors gain a viable path to seek monetary relief in the Court of Federal Claims when statutes use mandatory language directing payment.
impacts.negativeImplications: The Government faces potential significant liability when agencies fail to follow statutory mandates for distributing appropriated funds, even after those funds have been rescinded by subsequent legislation.

impacts.caseName: Willis Electric Co., Ltd. v. Polygroup Ltd. (Macao Commercial Offshore)
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court properly denied JMOL of obviousness and a new trial on damages for a patent covering pre-lit artificial trees with integrated mechanical and electrical trunk connections.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the denial of Polygroup's JMOL of obviousness and motion for new trial on damages regarding Willis's patent for pre-lit artificial trees with simultaneous mechanical and electrical connections. The court upheld the jury's finding that the prior art did not render the claimed invention obvious.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders in the consumer products space benefit from the court's deference to jury findings on non-obviousness, particularly where claimed innovations involve integrated functionality not taught by prior art combinations.
impacts.negativeImplications: Competitors face continued enforcement exposure for consumer product patents even when the underlying technology appears incremental, as juries may find non-obviousness based on the specific combination of features.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court abused its discretion in excluding a patentee's damages expert report and granting summary judgment for the accused infringer based on the absence of a damages remedy.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the exclusion of Exafer's damages expert report in a patent infringement case against Microsoft involving virtual network optimization technology. The court vacated the district court's summary judgment based on absence of a remedy and its denial of Exafer's motion to reopen discovery, finding the exclusion was an abuse of discretion.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders benefit from greater latitude in presenting damages evidence, as the Federal Circuit signaled that district courts should not exclude expert reports based on overly strict methodology requirements that effectively eliminate a patentee's case.
impacts.negativeImplications: Technology companies defending patent infringement claims may face broader damages theories at trial, as the Federal Circuit limits courts' ability to exclude damages experts on Daubert grounds.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the ITC properly entered a limited exclusion order against defaulting respondents under 19 U.S.C. § 1337(g)(1) based on presumed facts from the complaint, and whether Crocs's appeal of the no-violation finding against active respondents was timely.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit dismissed as untimely Crocs's appeal of the ITC's no-violation finding against active respondents who participated in the investigation regarding Crocs's 3D trademark registrations for its Classic Clog. The court affirmed the ITC's limited exclusion order against defaulting respondents, holding the Commission did not abuse its discretion under § 337(g)(1).
impacts.positiveImplications: Brand owners can still obtain limited exclusion orders against defaulting importers in Section 337 investigations even when the ITC finds no violation by participating respondents, providing at least partial trademark enforcement relief.
impacts.negativeImplications: Trademark holders must strictly comply with appeal deadlines from ITC final determinations, and a finding of no likelihood of confusion against active respondents does not extend protection against non-defaulting parties.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court abused its discretion in excluding the patent holder's expert witnesses on infringement and erred in granting JMOL of non-infringement for medical device patents covering spinal derotation techniques.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed JMOL of non-infringement and the exclusion of two expert witnesses in a patent case involving spinal surgery derotation tools against DePuy Synthes. The court found the district court abused its discretion in excluding expert testimony and remanded for a new trial where both experts may testify.
impacts.positiveImplications: Individual inventors and smaller patent holders benefit from the Federal Circuit's reversal, which limits district courts' ability to exclude technical experts in complex medical device cases, preserving access to trial on infringement claims.
impacts.negativeImplications: Medical device manufacturers face renewed litigation exposure when expert exclusion rulings that supported JMOL are reversed, potentially leading to costly retrials.

impacts.caseName: US Patent No. 7,679,637 LLC v. Google LLC
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patent claims directed to a web conferencing system with time-shifting capabilities for asynchronous viewing of multimedia presentations are patent-eligible under 35 U.S.C. § 101.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed dismissal of a patent infringement suit against Google, holding that claims to web conferencing systems with time-shifting and asynchronous playback capabilities failed to state a claim. The decision reinforces the application of § 101 to dismiss web-based technology patents at the pleading stage.
impacts.positiveImplications: Technology companies targeted by NPEs asserting web conferencing and streaming patents can seek early dismissal under § 101, reducing the cost and burden of patent litigation.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders in the web conferencing and collaboration technology space face heightened risk of early dismissal, limiting the value of such patent portfolios.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether registered nurses employed by the Indian Health Service under Title 5 who receive enhanced base pay under Title 38 are also entitled to Title 38's increased compensation rates for overtime, nighttime, Sunday, and holiday work.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the dismissal of a class action by IHS nurses seeking enhanced pay for overtime, nighttime, Sunday, and holiday work under both Title 38 and Title 5 compensation provisions. The court held that the nurses adequately stated claims for these pay enhancements and remanded for further proceedings on four of five counts.
impacts.negativeImplications: Federal agencies employing healthcare workers under hybrid Title 5/Title 38 compensation frameworks face potential class-wide liability for unpaid overtime, night, Sunday, and holiday pay differentials, with significant backpay exposure.
